steps.title: Identify the grammatical structure needed
steps.content: The sentence contains two independent clauses: "Many...were [blank]" and "historians have determined that Shakespeare's company did perform a play called Cardenio in 1613." The word "but" is the coordinating conjunction joining them. The rule for joining two independent clauses with a coordinating conjunction is: place a comma before the conjunction. The blank must end with "skeptical," followed by "but" to form a grammatical sentence.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'skeptical but': Joining two independent clauses with a coordinating conjunction alone (no comma) creates a run-on sentence — violates the comma-before-coordinating-conjunction rule. ✗
  • Choice B — 'skeptical, but': The comma before "but" correctly separates the two independent clauses, satisfying the rule for compound sentences. ✓
  • Choice C — 'skeptical,': A comma alone joining two independent clauses creates a comma splice — a coordinating conjunction is also required. ✗
  • Choice D — 'skeptical': No punctuation between the two independent clauses creates a fused (run-on) sentence. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
